narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 54% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including being in the 4th decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—lower than the typical student's 6th decile or so. Your LSAT score of 35 and undergraduate GPA of 2.6 also fall below the averages of 37 and 3.2 seen in students who usually pass the bar. As a female student from a middle-income family, planning to study full-time, and with a White racial background, your profile aligns closely with many applicants, but those demographics had minimal sway here. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your third-year school decile ranking, which pushed the prediction toward failure more than any other factor. Coming in second was your first-year decile, also nudging it toward expecting a struggle on the bar. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, similarly contributing to the higher failure risk. Family income level and undergraduate GPA followed closely, each adding a bit more weight toward that outcome, while gender, race, and full-time status had the least impact. Overall, your grades in later school years stood out as the top concerns compared to peers who pass, suggesting the model sees early and persistent academic challenges as key bar exam hurdles. Typical successful students have stronger deciles, higher test scores, and better GPAs that pull predictions safely toward passing. To improve your odds for future applications or bar prep, focus on what you can control: consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthen your GPA through additional coursework if possible, and build study habits that mirror top performers' consistent academic performance. With targeted effort on these areas, you could shift your profile closer to those who succeed and reapply stronger. Keep pushing—you have the potential to turn this around.
